derivation: DNS = Domain Name System; 'Dynamic' belongs to DHCP, and the protocol resolves names, not networks.
DHCP = Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol; 'Control' and 'Hyper' are the invented distractor words.
IPv4 address = 4 octets x 8 bits = 32 bits (2^32 ~ 4.3e9 addresses).
IPv6 address = 8 hextets x 16 bits = 128 bits.

myAnswer: T=0 -> Q(t+1)=Q(t) (hold); T=1 -> Q(t+1)=Q(t)' (toggle); Q(t+1) = T·Q(t)' + T'·Q(t) = T XOR Q(t)
derivation: Canonical T flip-flop characteristic table; read /figures/ce2016-q028-answer.png and it matches answerText verbatim (2-row table plus equation Qt+1 = T Qt' + T' Qt).
shippedAnswer: [T-FF] Truth Table: T=0 -> Q(t+1)=Q(t); T=1 -> Q(t+1)=Q(t)'; Equation -> Qt+1 = T Qt' + T' Qt

derivation: Canonical JK flip-flop table (SR with 1,1 redefined as toggle); read /figures/ce2016-q029-answer.png and it matches answerText verbatim including equation Qt+1 = J Qt' + K' Qt.
shippedAnswer: [JK-FF] Truth Table: 00->Q(t), 01->0, 10->1, 11->Q(t)'; Equation -> Qt+1 = J Qt' + K' Qt

derivation: Tie J and K together to T (a wire, no gate). Brute-forced: Q+ = J·Q' + K'·Q with J=K=T gives T XOR Q on all 4 rows, exactly the T-FF.
J=D, K=D' requires one inverter. Brute-forced: J=D,K=NOT D gives Q+=D on all 4 rows; tying D to both J and K with no gate gives D XOR Q and fails 2 of 4 rows, so 'no gate' (D) is wrong.

Computed: 1110 0000 = 224, 1100 0000 = 192, sum = 416 = 1_1010_0000. 8-bit result 1010 0000 with carry-out 1 (unsigned overflow). Note as two's complement -32 + -64 = -96, which fits in 8 bits, so there is no SIGNED overflow; the item means the carry-out bit.

WATS = Wide-Area Telephone Service, a real telecom communication service; DDT (insecticide/debugger) and DTS (audio codec) are noise. Caveat: DDD = Direct Distance Dialing is also a genuine telephone service, so option C is arguably defensible; the item is weak but A is the intended and strongest answer.

Forced choice: A (keys piling onto the same hash address) is the closest description of clustering. Strictly, two keys mapping to one address is a COLLISION, and B (probing for another slot when the address is taken) is the linear-probing mechanism that actually produces primary clustering. The item is loosely worded; A is the best of a poor option set but the distinction collision-vs-clustering is blurred.

ipconfig on the server already validates the server's own stack/addressing, so C (check server) is redundant and the fault lies on the path. tracert from host to server localises the failing hop; bare 'ping' (A) gives only pass/fail and 'ping gateway' (B) tests just the first hop. Stem is typo-ridden ('ipconfing') but answerable.

Counters advance by toggling bits; the T flip-flop toggles on each clock when T=1, so chained T-FFs form a ripple counter. A JK with J=K=1 behaves identically (so B is tempting), but T is the element defined for the job.
The circular/spiral rings of data on the disc surface are tracks. Sectors are angular subdivisions within a track; clusters are filesystem groups of sectors; headers are metadata fields.

Conventional keyed answer: Quick Sort averages O(n log n) with the smallest constant factors and sorts in place, so it is fastest in practice among the four. Caveat: 'best' is undefined here; heap sort is O(n log n) WORST case while quicksort degrades to O(n^2) with a bad pivot, so the item is arguable. B is the intended answer.
